steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: On the left side of the blank: an independent clause. On the right side: an introductory participial phrase followed by another independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the rule and choose the correct answer
steps.content:
Identify the structure on each side of the punctuation: left side is an independent clause ending at "economic ___"; right side begins with a participial phrase ("citing previously unpublished records...") that modifies the subject of the following independent clause. A participial phrase that begins a new clause must be preceded by strong punctuation separating it from the prior IC.
  • Choice A — might and: "...economic might and citing previously unpublished records..." — joins two ICs with just "and" but no comma, creating a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — might,: "...economic might, citing previously unpublished records..." — comma splice: two independent clauses joined by only a comma. ✗
  • Choice C — might by: "...economic might by citing previously unpublished records..." — turns the participial phrase into a prepositional modifier of "might," changing the meaning and producing a run-on. ✗
  • Choice D — might;: "...economic might; citing previously unpublished records, Auerbach makes..." — semicolon correctly separates the first IC from the second, which opens with an introductory participial phrase. ✓
That matches Choice D — might;.
